ontology.foodex2_description: Pork meat products — cooked/processed pork rib preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for barbecued spare ribs; A00LC (pork meat products) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for barbecued spare ribs as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of barbecued spare ribs is approximately 200 g (3–4 individual rib pieces), as commonly served in a restaurant or home setting.

nutrient_source.title: Barbecued Spare Ribs — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Barbecued Spare Ribs (cr_id F06100004). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat value of 11.5 g/100g is derived from USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 10124, Pork, fresh, loin, blade (chops or roasts), bone-in, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented as an adapted estimate. Potassium (280 mg/100g) is also an adapted estimate from USDA FoodData Central pork rib data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for barbecued spare ribs. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on the analogous food category of mixed meat dishes with added sugar-based sauce (GI ~40–50 range per University of Sydney GI Database for comparable sweet-glazed pork preparations). The carbohydrate content is low (5 g/100g), predominantly from the barbecue sauce glaze. GL per 200 g serving = (40 × 10 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.0. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this specific preparation has not been published.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (549 mg/100g) combined with high saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g) contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit barbecued spare ribs to a small occasional portion (80–100 g), request reduced-sodium marinades, and trim visible fat before consumption to reduce both sodium and saturated fat burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High saturated fat (11.5 g/100g) worsening insulin resistance and sugar-based glaze contributing to advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should strictly limit portion size, avoid honey- and sugar-based glazes that increase carbohydrate load, and be aware that the high saturated fat content independently impairs insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g) and high sodium (549 mg/100g) with high-heat barbecuing generating pro-atherogenic advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat barbecued spare ribs as an occasional indulgence only, limiting portions to 80 g or less, choosing leaner cuts where possible, and pairing with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the atherogenic lipid and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory combination of red meat, high saturated fat, high sodium, AGEs from high-heat barbecuing, and added sugar
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid barbecued spare ribs as the combination of red meat arachidonic acid (omega-6), high saturated fat, high-heat AGEs, and added sugar collectively amplifies inflammatory pathways; substitution with oily fish or plant-based protein is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Barbecued spare ribs are a popular pork dish consisting of pork spare rib sections marinated and cooked over high heat — typically grilled or barbecued — and finished with a sweet-savoury barbecue sauce glaze made from ingredients such as soy sauce, hoisin sauce, sugar or honey, garlic, and spices. The result is tender, juicy pork with a caramelised, slightly charred exterior and a rich, smoky-sweet flavour profile. The dish is widely consumed in Western, American, and international restaurant contexts, and is also common in Singapore hawker and casual dining settings. Per 100 g, barbecued spare ribs provide 398 kcal, 22.9 g protein, 31.9 g fat (of which approximately 11.5 g is saturated fat), 5.0 g carbohydrate (all from the glaze), 0.0 g dietary fibre, and 549 mg sodium. The high saturated fat and sodium content make this a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The high-heat barbecuing process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are independently pro-inflammatory and pro-atherogenic. A typical serving is approximately 200 g (3–4 rib pieces), delivering approximately 796 kcal and 1098 mg sodium — nearly half the recommended daily sodium intake for an adult. Clinically, this food should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by individuals with cardiometabolic or inflammatory conditions.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06100004, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central closest pork rib equivalent and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database category; this is clearly documented. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (11.5 g/100g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central (pork spare ribs, cooked) as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this adaptation is documented in nutrient_source. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific barbecued spare ribs preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (cr_id F06100004) nor in USDA FoodData Central for this exact Singapore-style barbecued preparation as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central were consulted; neither provides a complete micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this specific preparation. 3. potassium_mg (280 mg/100g) is an adapted estimate from USDA FoodData Central pork rib data and is used in the hypertension health context note. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — pork ribs with barbecue sauce glaze contain no dietary fibre. 5. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct peer-reviewed GI measurement for barbecued spare ribs has been published. 6. This dish is Western/American in origin, not a Singaporean-origin dish. Multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply; local_names fields are null accordingly. 7. Halal status is false as pork is not halal.
